title: ZTNA Application Access
dataSources: ZTNA access logs (application, user, device, action)
app: `Splunk_TA_zscaler` (ZPA), Netskope Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 3808), `Splunk_TA_paloalto` (Prisma Access), `TA-fortinet_fortigate` (FortiSASE), Check Point App for Splunk (Splunkbase 4293)
spl:
index=zt sourcetype="zscaler:zpa"
| stats dc(user) as unique_users, count as total_access by application
| sort -unique_users
description: Per-application access patterns in ZTNA reveal usage trends, security risks, and application performance issues.
value: Per-application access patterns in ZTNA reveal usage trends, security risks, and application performance issues.
implementation: Track application access through ZTNA per user and device. Identify unused applications for decommissioning. Monitor access patterns for anomalies. Report on application adoption and usage.
visualization: Bar chart (top applications by users), Table (application access summary), Line chart (access trends per app).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication
  by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src span=1h
| sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Anomalies from new SaaS app rollouts, browser updates, or vendor URL category changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_zscaler` (ZPA), Netskope Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 3808), `Splunk_TA_paloalto` (Prisma Access), `TA-fortinet_fortigate` (FortiSASE), Check Point App for Splunk (Splunkbase 4293).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: ZTNA access logs (application, user, device, action).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Track application access through ZTNA per user and device. Identify unused applications for decommissioning. Monitor access patterns for anomalies. Report on application adoption and usage.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=zt sourcetype="zscaler:zpa"
| stats dc(user) as unique_users, count as total_access by application
| sort -unique_users
```

Understanding this SPL

**ZTNA Application Access** — Per-application access patterns in ZTNA reveal usage trends, security risks, and application performance issues.

Documented **Data sources**: ZTNA access logs (application, user, device, action). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_zscaler` (ZPA), Netskope Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 3808), `Splunk_TA_paloalto` (Prisma Access), `TA-fortinet_fortigate` (FortiSASE), Check Point App for Splunk (Splunkbase 4293). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: zt; **sourcetype**: zscaler:zpa. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=zt, sourcetype="zscaler:zpa". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by application** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with Zscaler ZPA admin for the same applications, connectors, and time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Step 4 — Operationalize
Step 4 — Operationalize
Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (top applications by users), Table (application access summary), Line chart (access trends per app).
grandmaExplanation: We use network security tools to watch who accesses what, help prevent data leaks, enforce network isolation, and secure cloud and remote access.
